title: Leptochilus pedunculatus
intro: Leptochilus pedunculatus, commonly known as the Burdock plant, is a biennial plant in the Asteraceae family characterized by its large, coarse leaves and purple flowers. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including treating skin conditions such as eczema and acne, as well as reducing inflammation and improving digestion. The bioactive constituents of Leptochilus pedunculatus include arctiin, arctigenin, and luteolin, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Leptochilus pedunculatus is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as being a key ingredient in traditional remedies like Burdock root coffee and Burdock root capsules.
taxonomy: Leptochilus pedunculatus is a species of fern in the family Polypodiaceae, which is commonly known as the polypody fern family. It belongs to the order Polypodiales and the class Polypodiopsida, both of which are part of the division Pteridophyta. This classification places Leptochilus pedunculatus among the true ferns, a group of vascular plants that reproduce via spores. Leptochilus pedunculatus is native to tropical and subtropical regions, where it thrives in shaded, humid environments. By studying its classification, botanists can gain insights into the plant's evolutionary history and relationships with other fern species.

constituents: Leptochilus pedunculatus, commonly known as the devil's claw or grapple plant, is a plant that is traditionally used in herbal medicine.
